Wastewater Treatment - Unified Modeling Report

Generated 2026-04-29 11:58  ·  Experiments 1-4 + Phases 9-11  ·  9 models  ·  8 effluent targets  ·  Train 2021-2024 · Test 2025

Overview

Abstract

  • Baseline (Exp1 & 2): Established that inlet water quality alone is insufficient for prediction. Secondary clarifier data is essential to achieve meaningful accuracy.
  • Feature Expansion (Exp3): Aeration basin data (Exp3-S2) produced the best linearly-regularised models (ElasticNet/Ridge), providing the most robust baseline predicting 2025 holdout data.
  • Feature Pruning (Exp4): Removing collinear features (via VIF or manually) degraded generalisation across the board. The collinear features carry structural signal necessary for decision tree models, while regularised linear models already suppress collinearity automatically.
  • Advanced Methods (Ensembles & Temporal): Stacking and Neural Networks (ANN) struggled to generalise on the extremely small Composite datasets (n≈290). Conversely, temporal lag features successfully improved R² on Grab targets, demonstrating that recent flow history strongly influences spot samples.
  • Practical significance — R² alone is insufficient: A high Test R² does not guarantee compliance-grade accuracy. Discharge limits for BOD and TSS are 10 mg/L. Grab BOD (best R²≈0.69): RMSE ≈ 2 mg/L — roughly 20% of the limit, operationally useful. Grab TSS (best R²≈0.64): RMSE ≈ 6 mg/L — roughly 60% of the limit; the model explains variance well but its absolute prediction error is large relative to the compliance threshold. Predictions should be treated as trend indicators rather than compliance certifications for TSS.

Global Leaderboard - Best Result Per Target

Left half: the highest raw Test R² achieved for each target across all experiments - the naive ceiling. A ⚠ superscript on the Test R² value flags targets where the naive champion is overfit (gap ≥ 0.10). Right half: the gap-adjusted recommendation - the best model after penalising large train/test gaps (see Model Selection for the full rule explanation).

1 model fall within the ±0.05 R² competitive window: Exp3-KS · RF (R²=0.484, gap=+0.282). The smallest |gap| among them is +0.282 - every competitive model carries a substantial overfit gap. The gap-adjusted score (max 0.393) still peaks at the naive champion (Exp3-KS · RF), so no less-overfit alternative achieves comparable accuracy on this target. This target needs more training data, better features, or a regime-specific model rather than a different model from the current pool.

MdAE % Limit is computed from row-level predictions stored in dataset files. Feature Engineering and Temporal Features phases do not store row-level predictions, so MdAE shows - when their models are the recommended choice. BOD/TSS targets only — MdAE is not shown for pH (narrow range) or COD (no discharge limit).

Metric Progression Across Experiments

View how model performance evolved across experiments for specific targets. Gaps in a line indicate the model was not run in that experiment. R²: higher is better. RMSE / MAE: lower is better (original units, mg/L or pH).

Experiment 1 - Inlet Features

Experiment 1 investigates inlet-feature prediction across two feature-set scopes. Sub-experiment 1 uses only the 4 inlet concentration columns — the absolute floor. Sub-experiment 2 adds Flow, Power, and cyclic calendar features (month/dow encoded as sin/cos projections), raising the count to 11. All datasets use cyclic calendar encoding as the standard going forward. Feature selection is model-specific and built into each training run: OLS uses LassoCV pre-screening, Ridge regularises over the full set (no pruning), ElNet applies L1 selection internally, and tree models (RF, GB, XGB) use OOF permutation importance to retain features above a 5% relative importance threshold. The Sub-experiment Comparison panel at the bottom shows the full-feature vs feature-selected breakdown per model and target with magnitude-weighted deltas.

▶ Sub-experiment 1 — Inlet Only (4 features, no FS)
Feature Set
Inlet Only (4 features) — no process or temporal context
Columns: Inlet pH, Inlet BOD (mg/L), Inlet COD (mg/L), Inlet TSS (mg/L) (Grab or Composite) — no Flow, Power, or calendar features
Hypothesis: Absolute minimum feature set: can inlet water quality alone predict effluent quality? This sub-experiment establishes the floor and quantifies how much process context (Flow, Power, calendar) adds when compared against Exp1 (Sub 2).

Comp TSS-13.48413.59121.41216.36011.541-0.2780.2936.3605.7855.859★ -0.2190.2356.2115.6175.631-4.7995.04913.5488.2123.834-4.6085.13413.3249.4796.442-5.2315.72914.0448.5073.759

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

▶ Sub-experiment Comparison — Sub1 / Sub2 Full / Sub2 Feature-Selected

Sub1 (inlet only, 4 features, no FS) → Sub2 Full (cyclic calendar + process, 11 features, no pruning) → Sub2 FS (same 11 features, model-specific selection applied). For linear models, Full and FS differ only for OLS (LassoCV may prune); Ridge keeps the full set (L2 handles collinearity), ElNet runs L1 on the full set. For tree models, Full = Phase 1 CV result (all features); FS = Phase 3 result after OOF permutation importance pruning (≥5% threshold). ★ = best raw Test R² per (model, target) · ✦ = best gap-adjusted score · green = both · orange = raw only · blue = gap-adj only · green Δ = improvement · red Δ = regression. RMSE reported in native units (mg/L or pH units). Gap = Train R² − Test R²; red Gap > 0.10 = notable overfit.

Feature Selection Validation — Sub-experiment 2 (11 cyclic features input)

Each model applies a distinct selection protocol to the shared 11-feature pool. The table confirms which method was used, how many features were retained on average across the 8 targets, and whether the protocol was correctly applied.

Model FS Method Input
Feats		 Avg Selected
(across 8 targets)		 Status
OLSLassoCV — L1 cross-validated pre-screen (TimeSeriesSplit)118.1 / 11✓ Selection applied
RidgeFull set — L2 regularisation handles collinearity internally1111 / 11 (all)✓ Correctly bypassed (L2)
ElNetInternal L1 — retains features with non-zero fitted coefficients112.9 / 11✓ Selection applied
RFOOF permutation importance ≥ 5% threshold (3-phase: full → select → refit)114.8 / 11✓ Selection applied
GBOOF permutation importance ≥ 5% threshold (3-phase: full → select → refit)115.2 / 11✓ Selection applied
XGBOOF permutation importance ≥ 5% threshold (3-phase: full → select → refit)115.2 / 11✓ Selection applied

Ridge is the only model that intentionally bypasses feature removal — L2 shrinks uninformative coefficients toward zero without discarding them. All other models reduce the feature space. Per-target selected feature lists are in the Model-Specific Feature Selection fold within Sub-experiment 2 above.

Transition Summary

Transition Net Mean ΔR²
raw		 Improvements
Δ > +0.01		 Regressions
Δ < −0.01		 Negligible
|Δ| ≤ 0.01		 Gap-Adj Net ΔR²
R²−0.5·max(0,|gap|−0.10)		 Gap-Adj − Raw
overfitting shift		 Verdict / Interpretation
Sub1 → Sub2 Full-0.321732/48 (avg +0.471)12/48 (avg -2.542)4/48-0.5482-0.2264Net regression
Raw gains partially inflated by increased overfitting
Sub2 Full → Sub2 FS-0.38264/48 (avg +0.262)20/48 (avg -0.971)24/48-0.5379-0.1553Net regression
Raw gains partially inflated by increased overfitting

Reading the table. Net Mean ΔR² is the signed average across all 48 (model × target) cells. Sub2 Full = full 11-feature set before any pruning (OLS: unregularised on all features; Ridge/ElNet: full set; RF/GB/XGB: Phase 1 CV model). Sub2 FS = model-specific selection applied (OLS: LassoCV; Ridge: no change — L2 handles it; ElNet: no change — L1 selects internally; RF/GB/XGB: OOF permutation importance refit on features with ≥5% relative importance). Gap-Adj − Raw indicates whether raw gains are inflated by increased overfitting (negative = gains overstated) or understated by reduced overfitting (positive = gains understated). ★ = best raw Test R² · ✦ = best gap-adjusted score · green = both · orange = raw only · blue = gap-adj only. In the delta columns: green ΔR² = improvement · red ΔR² = regression · red ΔGap = gap widened (more overfit) · green ΔGap = gap narrowed (less overfit) · grey = negligible (|Δ| ≤ 0.01).

▶ Findings — Experiment 1
Q1 Do inlet concentrations alone carry meaningful predictive power?

Barely. Only 6/48 (model × target) evaluations achieved a positive Test R². Best single result: OLS on Grab BOD (R² = +0.185). Mean Test R² across all cells: -0.879. All composite targets and Grab COD returned universally negative R² — inlet concentrations carry no generalisable signal for those targets. This establishes the floor: inlet data is necessary but not sufficient.

Q2 What does adding process context (Flow, Power, cyclic calendar) contribute?

Both model families degraded. Adding 7 process/calendar features increased parameter count without proportional signal gain at this dataset size — OLS especially suffers from overfitting with 11 unconstrained coefficients.
Linear models (Sub2 Full): mean Δ = -1.110 overall (Grab: -0.345, Composite: -1.875).
Tree models: mean Δ = -0.299 overall (Grab: -0.012, Composite: -0.585). Ridge and ElNet are the meaningful linear references here — OLS without regularisation is poorly suited to 11 features and its column in the comparison table largely reflects overfitting, not the actual signal in the feature set.

Q3 Does model-specific feature selection improve accuracy or generalisation within Sub2?

Model-specific FS reduced mean Test R² overall (20/48 cells regressed).
Overall: mean Δ = -0.383 (Grab: -0.062, Composite: -0.703).
Generalisation: Mean Δ R²-gap (FS − Full) = -0.009 (1/24 model-target pairs saw the gap narrow).
By model: linear +0.009, tree -0.774.
For OLS: LassoCV kept all 11 features in most cases (regularisation path found no features to zero out), so Full ≈ FS. When features were pruned, it benefited generalization only modestly. Ridge does not prune — L2 distributes weight across all features. Full = FS. ElNet's L1 penalty selects internally on the full set — Full = FS by construction.
Tree OOF FS (threshold ≥5% relative importance): mean Δ for trees = -0.774. At 11 features × ~800–1175 training rows, the OOF selection step often hurt rather than helped — the pruned features still contributed signal, and the refit on a reduced set introduced a different overfit pattern.
Implication: at the Exp1 feature-set level (11 features), model-specific FS does not consistently improve on using the full set. Feature selection becomes more valuable when the feature pool grows larger relative to the training size (Exp3 and beyond).

Q4 Which variant achieves the best performance per target?

Raw winner: Sub2 FS (1 Sub1 / 5 Sub2-Full / 6 Sub2-FS ★). Gap-adjusted winner: Sub2 Full (1 Sub1 / 7 Sub2-Full / 4 Sub2-FS ✦). Raw and gap-adjusted winners agree on all targets. The table below shows the best raw Test R² (large) and gap-adjusted score (small, grey) each variant achieves per target:

Target Sub1 Sub2 Full Sub2 FS

-0.172

+0.101		+0.136★✦
+0.122		+0.136★✦
+0.122

-0.852		-0.515★✦

★ = raw R² winner · ✦ = gap-adj winner · green = both · orange = raw only · blue = gap-adj only. Small grey value = gap-adjusted score.

Q5 Do Grab and Composite targets respond differently to feature changes?

Consistently, yes. Grab targets respond more positively to every transition.
Sub1 → Sub2 Full: Grab -0.179, Composite -1.230. Full → FS: Grab -0.062, Composite -0.703. The pattern is structural: Composite targets have roughly 800 training rows vs ~1,175 for Grab. Any feature change that expands dimensionality amplifies overfitting risk proportionally more on the smaller composite sets. Composite COD and pH fail across all variants — they need secondary clarifier data (Experiment 2 and beyond) before any model generalises.

Best Model in Experiment 1 avg best Test R² = -0.249 · Grab avg -0.031 · Composite avg -0.468

gap-adj below		 GapSource (gap-adj winner)

raw: XGB -0.057 (Sub2 FS Cyclic · XGB (OOF-sel))

gap-adj +0.122		-0.127Sub2 · Ridge (L2 full)

gap-adj -0.657		—Sub2 Full Cyclic · XGB

Selection: winner chosen by gap-adjusted score = R² − 0.5 · max(0, |gap| − 0.10), consistent with the global leaderboard methodology. Where the raw R² winner differs from the gap-adj winner it is shown in orange below the source label. avg best Test R² uses the raw per-target best for cross-experiment comparability. Composite averages should be read cautiously — all composite targets fail at the Exp1 feature level (Grab COD likewise); secondary clarifier data is required before these targets become learnable.

Experiment 2 - Secondary & Combined Features

Experiment 2 expands the feature set from inlet-only to include secondary treatment data. Sub-experiment 1 tests three secondary-only feature scopes side by side: Sec Clarifier features alone (10 features), Sec Sedimentation features alone (10 features), and both groups combined (15 features). The split variants test whether the two secondary groups carry distinct signal or are interchangeable. Sub-experiment 2 adds inlet concentrations to the combined secondary set (21 features) and applies model-specific feature selection (OLS: LassoCV; trees: OOF permutation importance).

▶ Sub-experiment 1 — Secondary Features Only (three scopes)

Three feature scopes tested side by side within Sub-experiment 1. Combined (15 features) is the original baseline — both secondary groups together. Clarifier-only and Sed-only (10 features each) isolate each group to test whether they carry distinct signal or are interchangeable. All variants include COMMON (Flow, Power, year, month_sin/cos, dow_sin/cos). Co-occurring missingness means row counts are nearly identical across the three.

▶ Sec Clarifier + COMMON (10 features)
Feature Set
Secondary Clarifier + COMMON (12 features)
Columns: Sec Clarifier pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS + Flow, Power, year, month_sin, month_cos, dow_sin, dow_cos
Hypothesis: Isolate the Clarifier group to test whether Clarifier and Sedimentation features carry distinct signal or are interchangeable.

▶ Sub-experiment 2 — Inlet + Secondary + COMMON (21 features, model-specific FS integrated)
Feature Set
Inlet + Secondary + COMMON (21 features)
Columns: Grab or Composite inlet (4) + all SEC_COLS (10) + Flow, Power, year, month_sin, month_cos, dow_sin, dow_cos (7)
Hypothesis: Full combined feature set with corrected calendar encoding. OLS uses LassoCV pre-screening; trees use OOF permutation importance feature selection, both stored in the same results file.
▶ Dataset Details (n_train / n_test / n_features)
Targetn_train (2021-2024) n_test (2025)n_features

▶ Model-Specific Feature Selection

Green = selected by all model classes (universal core)  |  Amber = model-specific selection  |  OLS: LassoCV pre-screen (TimeSeriesSplit)  |  ElasticNet: internal L1 selection (non-zero coefficients)  |  RF/GB/XGB: OOF permutation importance

Reading the table. Sub1-Clr = Sec Clarifier + COMMON (10 features, no FS). Sub1-Sed = Sec Sedimentation + COMMON (10 features, no FS). Combined = both secondary groups + COMMON (15 features, original baseline). Sub2 Full = Inlet + Secondary + COMMON (21 features, no FS applied; for OLS this is the pre-LassoCV result). Sub2 FS = same 21 features after model-specific selection (OLS: LassoCV; Ridge: full set; ElNet: internal L1; RF/GB/XGB: OOF permutation importance ≥5%). ★ = best raw Test R² · ✦ = best gap-adjusted score. Detailed transition deltas (win/loss counts and gap-adjusted net) are in the Transition Summary below.

▶ Findings — Experiment 2
Q1 Do Sec Clarifier and Sec Sedimentation features carry distinct signal, or are they interchangeable?

Sedimentation features slightly outperform Clarifier (avg Sed=+0.048 vs Clarifier=-0.384; Δ=+0.432). The difference is small but consistent across targets.
Grab targets: Sed − Clr mean Δ = +0.358, Composite targets: Sed − Clr mean Δ = +0.505. Co-occurring missingness means both groups have nearly identical row counts — row count is not a confound here. The signal overlap is high because Clarifier and Sedimentation units process the same water sequentially.

Q2 What does combining both secondary groups add over either individually?

Adding Sedimentation to Clarifier (Clr→Combined): mean Δ = +0.284 (42/48 cells improved).
Adding Clarifier to Sedimentation (Sed→Combined): mean Δ = -0.148.
Grab: +0.341, Composite: +0.226. The combined group rarely beats the better individual group by more than a small margin — confirming the high signal overlap between the two secondary sub-systems. Models using the combined 15-feature set do not consistently outperform either 10-feature variant because regularised models (Ridge, ElNet) already handle the redundancy, and tree models are not noticeably improved by the additional 5 features.

Q3 What does adding inlet concentrations contribute (Combined → Sub2-FS)?

Combined (15) → Sub2-FS (21 feat + FS): mean Δ = +0.028 (13/48 improved, 23/48 regressed).
Grab: -0.057, Composite: +0.112. OLS uses LassoCV to reduce the 21-feature set; tree models use OOF permutation importance (≥5% threshold) to select the most informative features. Where the delta is positive, the inlet signal and correct calendar encoding add genuine predictive value. Where negative, the added features primarily increase variance (especially on smaller composite datasets, n≈730–740 train rows).

Q4 Do Grab and Composite targets respond differently to the secondary feature transitions?

Yes — consistently.
Clarifier-only: Grab avg = -0.054, Composite avg = -0.714.
Sed-only: Grab avg = +0.304, Composite avg = -0.208. Grab targets respond better to every secondary-feature configuration. Composite targets show mostly negative or near-zero R² from secondary-only features — they need inlet concentrations (Sub2) before any linear model generalises. The structural reason: composite samples are collected on fewer days (~730–970 rows) and their effluent quality is driven more by inlet load (what goes in) than by instantaneous secondary process readings. Composite COD fails across all Exp2 variants — this target requires either additional features or a process-specific explanation.

Best Model in Experiment 2 avg best Test R² = 0.415 · Grab avg 0.500 · Composite avg 0.330

⚠ Overfitting note: Comp COD show |gap| > 0.25 even on the gap-adj winner — treat these results with caution.

Selection: winner chosen by gap-adjusted score = R² − 0.5 · max(0, |gap| − 0.10), consistent with the global leaderboard methodology. Where the raw R² winner differs from the gap-adj winner it is shown in orange below the source label. avg best Test R² uses the raw per-target best for cross-experiment comparability.

Experiment 3 — Feature Audit Tiers & Kitchen Sink

Experiment 3 starts from the Exp2-Sub2 baseline (Inlet + Secondary + COMMON, 21 features) and adds features in three steps guided by the EDA Feature Audit.

Sub-experiment 1 (ADD-tier, 28 features) adds the seven aeration-basin measurements that scored MI ≥ 0.20 with marginal row cost ≤ 20 %: Aeration DO/MLSS/SV30/SVI (Existing), Aeration pH (Existing), Aeration DO/SV30 (New). Grab training rows stay near ~816 — no meaningful row loss. No feature selection applied.

Sub-experiment 2 (ADD + CONSIDER-tier, 32 features, with model-specific FS) adds four CONSIDER-tier features (Aeration SVI New, Power GE, Inlet Total Coliform, Primary Sludge Totalizer). Inlet Total Coliform alone causes ~347 row loss (816 → 469 Grab). Feature selection (OLS: LassoCV; trees: OOF permutation importance) is applied inside training scripts to identify the most predictive subset.

Sub-experiment 3 (All remaining features, 44/39, with and without FS) includes every non-leakage column — the kitchen-sink upper bound. Joint dropna reduces Grab training to ~130 rows (74 % loss vs Exp2-Sub2) driven by TKN, Grit Classifier TSS, Primary COD/TSS. Both no-FS (Exp3-KS) and FS (Exp3-KS-FS) variants are evaluated. This sub-experiment confirms the audit's core finding: marginal row cost is the binding constraint for grab targets.

▶ Sub-experiment 1 — ADD-tier Aeration Features (28 features, no FS)
Feature Set
Exp2-S2 + ADD-tier Aeration Features (28 features, no FS)
Columns: Exp2-S2 baseline (21 features) + ADD-tier additions (MI ≥ 0.20, marginal row cost ≤ 20%): Aeration DO/MLSS/SV30/SVI (Existing) + Aeration pH (Existing) + Aeration DO/SV30 (New) — 7 extra columns. Grab: ~816 train rows / 202 test. Composite: ~632 train / 181 test.
Hypothesis: Expand with aeration basin data that shows high mutual information and low missingness cost on top of the Exp2-S2 baseline. No feature selection applied — tests the full ADD-tier set.

▶ Sub-experiment 2 — ADD + CONSIDER-tier with FS (LassoCV / OOF perm. importance)
Feature Set
Exp2-S2 + ADD + CONSIDER-tier Features with FS (32 features → 5–24 selected)
Columns: Exp2-S2 + ADD-tier + CONSIDER-tier (Aeration SVI New, Power GE, Inlet Total Coliform, Primary Sludge Totalizer) — 32 features total. Feature selection: OLS uses LassoCV; trees use OOF permutation importance. Grab: ~469 train rows / 168 test (Coliform causes ~347-row loss vs Exp3-S1). Composite: ~423 train / 152 test.
Hypothesis: Tests whether CONSIDER-tier features (higher missingness cost) add value when combined with model-specific feature selection. Key trade-off: CONSIDER features increase signal but reduce training rows significantly.
▶ Dataset Details (n_train / n_test / n_features)
Targetn_train (2021-2024) n_test (2025)n_features

Green = selected by all model classes (universal core)  |  Amber = model-specific selection  |  OLS: LassoCV pre-screen (TimeSeriesSplit)  |  ElasticNet: internal L1 selection (non-zero coefficients)  |  RF/GB/XGB: OOF permutation importance

▶ Sub-experiment 3 — All Remaining Features, no FS (44/39 features)
Feature Set
All remaining features — kitchen sink, no FS (44 Grab / 39 Composite features)
Columns: Exp2-S2 baseline + every non-leakage, non-redundant column from All_Years_Full.xlsx including TKN, NH3-N, Fecal/Total Coliform, Grit Classifier TSS, Primary COD/TSS, Power GE/NEA, Primary Sludge Totalizer. Grab: ~130 train rows / 115 test (74 % row loss vs Exp2-S2 due to joint missingness). Composite: ~498 train / 169 test.
Hypothesis: Establishes the ceiling of feature availability. Tests whether adding all available measurements, despite severe row loss, improves prediction. No feature selection applied — exposes raw overfitting risk at n/p ≈ 3.
▶ Dataset Details (n_train / n_test / n_features)

▶ Sub-experiment 3b — All Remaining Features with FS
Feature Set
All remaining features — kitchen sink with model-specific FS
Columns: Same 44/39 kitchen-sink features as Exp3-KS, with model-specific feature selection applied inside training scripts: OLS uses LassoCV; trees use OOF permutation importance (threshold=0.05). Selected: 2–24 features per target (OLS collapses to 1–3 on composites).
Hypothesis: Tests whether data-driven feature selection can rescue the undersized kitchen-sink dataset (n_train_grab ≈ 130). OOF fold size at n=130 with 3-fold TSS is only ~43 rows — permutation importance estimates are noisy at this scale.
▶ Dataset Details — Per-Model (n_train post-FS / n_test / n_features selected)

OLS and tree models (RF/GB/XGB) rebuild from All_Years_Full.xlsx after feature selection — rows previously lost to joint missingness of dropped features are recovered. Ridge/ElNet use the full KS feature set (no expansion). n_features = selected count for FS models; full KS count for Ridge/ElNet.

TargetModel n_train (post-FS rebuild) n_test (2025)n_features selected

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Inline deltas are shown for feature-selected variants only. For Test R², positive Δ means the FS model improved. For Gap, RMSE, MAE, and MdAE, positive Δ means the metric decreased versus the full-feature baseline.

Test R²: ≥0.6 strong · 0.4-0.6 good · 0.2-0.4 moderate · 0-0.2 weak · <0 fails baseline. Best per row: ★ highest Test R².

R² Gap (Train − Test): ■ <0.10 OK · ■ 0.10-0.25 mild overfit · ■ >0.25 severe overfit - treat result with caution.

MdAE (Median Absolute Error) shown for BOD and TSS targets only - RMSE is unreliable for these targets due to severe outlier distributions (up to 9.8% severe outliers in training data, TSS train max = 1266 mg/L). MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Inline deltas are shown for feature-selected variants only. For Test R², positive Δ means the FS model improved. For Gap, RMSE, MAE, and MdAE, positive Δ means the metric decreased versus the full-feature baseline.

▶ Sub-experiment Comparison — S1 / S2-FS / KS / KS-FS

S1 (ADD-tier, 28 features, ~816 Grab train rows) → S2-FS (ADD+CONSIDER, 32 features → model-selected, ~469 rows) → KS (all 44/39 features, ~130 Grab rows, no FS) → KS-FS (same data, model-specific FS applied). ★ = best raw Test R² per (model, target) · ✦ = best gap-adjusted · green = both · orange = raw only · blue = gap-adj only. RMSE in native units (mg/L or pH). Gap = Train R² − Test R²; red Gap > 0.10 = notable overfit.

Transition Summary

Transition Net Mean ΔR²
raw		 Improvements
Δ > +0.01		 Regressions
Δ < −0.01		 Negligible
|Δ| ≤ 0.01		 Gap-Adj Net ΔR²
R²−0.5·max(0,|gap|−0.10)		 Gap-Adj − Raw
overfitting shift		 Verdict
S1 (ADD) → S2-FS (ADD+CONSIDER)-0.008732/48 (avg +0.388)16/48 (avg -0.802)0/48+0.0573+0.0661Negligible
Raw gains understated — overfitting decreased
KS (no FS) → KS-FS-0.082814/48 (avg +0.704)18/48 (avg -0.768)16/48-0.0852-0.0024Net regression
Overfitting largely unchanged

Reading the table. Net Mean ΔR² is the signed average across all 48 (model × target) cells. S1 → S2-FS: adds CONSIDER-tier features (32 total) with model-specific FS on ~469 Grab train rows (−43 % vs S1's ~816). KS → KS-FS: applies FS to the 44/39-feature kitchen-sink set on ~130 Grab rows. Gap-Adj − Raw: negative = raw gains inflated by overfitting; positive = gains understated because overfitting decreased. ★ = best raw Test R² · ✦ = best gap-adjusted score · green = both · orange = raw only · blue = gap-adj only.

▶ Key Findings — Experiment 3
Q1 How did each sub-experiment's feature set affect sample size and performance?

Sub-exp 1 (ADD-tier) retains ~816 Grab training rows — negligible row loss vs Exp2-Sub2 (~470 after secondary-data filtering). The 7 aeration ADD features have 0–12 % marginal row cost because secondary data availability already gates the dataset. Best ADD Grab avg = +0.451; best ADD Comp avg = +0.099. RF avg Grab gap = +0.498 — moderate overfitting with 28 features at n=816.

Sub-exp 2 (ADD+CONSIDER) adds four CONSIDER-tier features. Inlet Total Coliform alone cuts Grab rows from ~816 to ~469 (−43 %). With FS applied, best S2-FS Grab avg = +0.495, Comp avg = +0.320.

Sub-exp 3 (kitchen sink) dropped Grab rows to ~130 (−74 % vs Exp2-Sub2). With n/p ≈ 3, OLS avg gap = +2.527, RF avg gap = +0.665. Kitchen-sink Grab avg (best per target) = +0.324, Comp avg = +0.149.

Q2 Did ADD+CONSIDER (Sub-exp 2) and kitchen-sink FS (Sub-exp 3) improve on ADD-only?

ADD → ADD+CONSIDER+FS: Mixed results. CONSIDER-tier features trade ~347 Grab training rows for four extra columns. Whether FS recovers the loss depends on target: OLS benefits (LassoCV prunes to the strongest ADD features), while tree models see mixed outcomes because OOF fold size at n=469 is ~156 rows — still workable but reduced.

Kitchen-sink FS (Sub-exp 3): Did not rescue the undersized grab dataset. The OOF fold size on 130 rows (3-fold TSS) is ~43 rows — permutation importance estimates are too noisy at this scale, causing the FS algorithm to prune to only 2–7 features per target, an overly aggressive cut. Composite targets (n_train ≈ 498) benefited more from FS than grab targets.

Q3 What is the takeaway for feature engineering strategy?

The Feature Audit tiers (ADD / CONSIDER) exist precisely to avoid the kitchen-sink trap. ADD-tier features (MI ≥ 0.20, row cost ≤ 20 %) give the best feature-count-to-row-cost ratio — Sub-exp 1 is the recommended baseline for Exp3. CONSIDER-tier features can help individual targets if FS is applied, but Inlet Total Coliform's 43 % Grab row loss is a steep price that must be weighed per-target.

The kitchen-sink sub-experiment (Sub-exp 3) confirms the audit's core conclusion: data-driven FS cannot rescue an undersized dataset — the FS algorithm itself operates in a high-variance regime when fold sizes are below ~50 rows. Feature selection should happen before the experiment based on MI signal and row cost, not purely inside the training script.

▶ Collinearity Diagnosis — Why Exp4 Was Designed

The kitchen-sink feature set (Sub-exp 3) contains several correlated groups (Sec Clarifier ↔ Sec Sedimentation; Aeration MLSS ↔ SVI; Power GE + Power NEA = Power Total). VIF analysis below quantifies this for OLS. Key context: VIF matters primarily for OLS (unregularised linear regression), where high VIF inflates coefficient standard errors and causes instability. Ridge and ElasticNet handle collinearity by design via their L2/L1 penalties, and tree models (RF, GB, XGB) are entirely unaffected — correlated features simply share split importance without degrading prediction.

This collinearity observation motivated Experiment 4: a direct test of whether manually removing the most collinear groups (SVI, New aeration, Sec Sed) would improve generalisation. Spoiler: it did not — see Exp4 findings.

▶ VIF Collinearity Analysis - Exp3-S2 Feature Sets

Variance Inflation Factor computed on training rows (year < 2025) after listwise deletion. VIF > 10 = problematic collinearity for linear models. VIF 5-10 = moderate. The threshold rule of thumb: VIF > 10 indicates that the variance of a coefficient estimate is inflated by a factor of 10 relative to an orthogonal design - OLS/Ridge predictions are less reliable for such features.

Global across all 8 datasets: 40 HIGH (VIF>10) · 80 MODERATE (VIF 5-10). Concentrated in Sec Sed vs Sec Clarifier pairs (adjacent measurements of the same parameter) and Aeration SVI (mathematically derived from SV30/MLSS). ElasticNet handles collinearity via L1 penalty - this explains why ElNet outperforms OLS/Ridge on composite targets. OLS/Ridge coefficients are directly inflated by collinear pairs.

Notes on VIF removal
  • Hub pattern: a feature appearing in many "Collinear With" rows (e.g. SVI) is a hub - dropping it alone resolves multiple HIGH VIFs simultaneously. Multiple HIGH flags ≠ remove all; count the distinct hubs first.
  • Tree models (RF, XGB) are unaffected - collinearity only inflates OLS/Ridge coefficient variance. ElasticNet handles it automatically via L1 shrinkage. VIF-based removal is relevant only before fitting OLS or Ridge.

⚠ Overfitting note: Grab pH, Comp COD show R² gap > 0.25. Best is ranked by Test R² among non-overfit models (gap ≤ 0.25) where available. High-gap winners may reflect the model memorising 2021-2024 patterns absent in 2025 - prefer lower-gap alternatives for deployment.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Experiment 4 - Collinearity Pruning

Experiment 4 tests the hypothesis that removing collinear and derived features from Exp3-S2 improves generalisation. Sub-experiment 1 manually drops three redundant groups (SVI, New aeration, Sec Sed - feature count 12-19). Sub-experiment 2 applies automated iterative VIF pruning (threshold=10) within the Exp4-S1 feature pool, reducing to 5-10 features per target and gaining +47 to +293 rows by dropping high-missingness correlated features. Overall result: both sub-experiments refuted the pruning hypothesis. S1 showed performance degraded universally. S2 confirmed the pattern: Ridge alone partially recovered on some targets; tree models failed catastrophically. See the finding boxes below for per-sub-experiment analysis.

▶ Sub-experiment 1 - Manual Redundancy Pruning (SVI, New Aeration, Sec Sed)
Feature Set
Exp3-S2 minus Derived/Redundant Columns (12-19 features)
Columns: Exp3-S2 feature set with three redundant groups removed: (1) Aeration SVI (Existing + New) - derived from SV30/MLSS, zero independent information; (2) All (New) aeration tank columns - cross-tank r=0.74-0.84, Existing tank wins on every BOD/COD/TSS target; (3) All Sec Sedimentation columns - cross-stage r=0.69-0.87, Sec Clarifier wins consistently. Remaining: Inlet, COMMON, Existing aeration (MLSS, SV30, DO, pH), Sec Clarifier (pH, TSS, BOD, COD, RAS).
Hypothesis: Hypothesis: removing collinear and derived features reduces variance inflation and improves generalisation, especially on composite targets where Exp3-S2 GB/XGB catastrophically overfit. Phase 1 of Exp4 - Phase 2 will address within-group VIF (MLSS vs SV30, Sec Clarifier inter-correlations).

⚠ Sub-exp 1 finding - manual pruning hypothesis refuted: Removing the three feature groups made performance worse across every target and every model. Row counts barely changed (+5-10, <2%) because Sec Sed and Sec Clarifier are collected on the same operational days (co-occurring missingness).

Why performance degraded:
  • Linear (ElNet/Ridge): regularisation already handled collinearity via L1/L2. Manual removal discarded signal the penalties would have downweighted automatically.
  • Tree models (RF, GB, XGB): Sec Sed COD/TSS carried direct process signal (RF Grab COD: +0.40 → −0.08). Fewer features → less split diversity → faster overfitting.
▶ Sub-experiment 2 - Automated VIF Pruning (threshold = 10)
Feature Set
Exp4-S1 + Iterative VIF Pruning (threshold=10) - 5-10 features
Columns: Starting from the Exp4-S1 feature pool, automated iterative VIF pruning (drop highest-VIF feature, recompute, repeat until all VIF ≤ 10) run on training rows only. Dropped per target: all pH features (Inlet pH, Sec Clarifier pH, Aeration pH, Primary Clarifier pH - VIF 600-2400), Inlet BOD/COD (VIF 18-34), MLSS (SV30 survives as biomass proxy), one of Flow/Power Total. Temporal features (month, day_of_week, year) always retained, excluded from VIF. Row gain vs Exp4-S1: +47 to +293 rows (dropped features had high missingness).
Hypothesis: Hypothesis: automated, data-driven VIF pruning removes intra-group multicollinearity more precisely than manual feature removal (Exp4-S1), yielding a well-conditioned feature matrix where Ridge/ElNet regularisation can work effectively and tree model overfitting may reduce.

▶ Train-set R² (reference)
TargetOLS Train R²Ridge Train R²ElNet Train R²RF Train R²GB Train R²XGB Train R²
Grab BOD0.6830.4650.1430.3090.8390.881
Grab COD0.6760.6620.5210.6370.8220.810
Grab TSS0.6720.4560.1600.3130.8840.854
Grab pH0.1950.1910.0940.3690.5580.451
Comp BOD0.6270.4230.1400.3200.8520.830
Comp COD0.5720.5720.5640.3520.7010.561
Comp TSS0.6400.4360.1630.2970.9240.919
Comp pH0.1770.0310.0000.5960.6020.668
⚠ Sub-exp 2 finding - VIF pruning also refuted, with nuance: Automated iterative VIF pruning (drop highest-VIF feature until all VIF ≤ 10) reduced feature sets to 5-10 and unlocked +47 to +293 additional rows by eliminating high-missingness correlated features (pH columns, Inlet BOD/COD, MLSS).

Row gain is significant for composites: Comp TSS grew from 448 → 741 rows (+65%), Comp COD from 684 → 866 (+27%). This is a genuine data efficiency gain.

But performance still degraded:
  • Ridge: mixed results - Grab BOD Ridge recovered to +0.463 (close to Exp4-S1 +0.518), Grab TSS Ridge stable at +0.373. But Comp pH Ridge collapsed to −1.44 and Comp COD remains unresolved.
  • ElNet: Grab BOD dropped to +0.072 (vs Exp3-S2 +0.684). ElNet relies on L1 to select from correlated features; pre-selecting them via VIF removes its advantage.
  • Tree models (RF, GB, XGB): catastrophic on most targets. Removing all pH features and Inlet BOD/COD strips the primary process-signal columns that trees use for their first splits. Without these, boosted trees cannot learn the systematic trend and overfit severely to noise.

Root cause: VIF pruning is target-agnostic - it removes features that are correlated with other features, regardless of their correlation with the target. Inlet BOD predicts Effluent BOD. Sec Clarifier pH predicts Effluent pH. These features have high VIF precisely because the plant operates systematically (pH is buffered across all stages), but they carry the predictive signal the models need.

Conclusion: Both manual and automated collinearity pruning are counter-productive on this dataset. The collinearity is intrinsic to the process physics and should be absorbed by regularisation (Ridge/ElNet), not removed. Tree models require the full feature pool. Exp3-S2 remains the best feature set for tree models. For linear models, Ridge on Exp4-S2 is competitive for Grab BOD/TSS but should not replace Exp3-S2 Ridge as the baseline due to losses on other targets.

Three-way comparison: Exp3-S2 → Exp4-S1 → Exp4-S2

Colours vs Exp3-S2 baseline: green = +0.02 gain, red = −0.02 loss, white = within ±0.02. Gap column: red = overfitting gap >0.15.

Model Exp3-S2 (baseline) Exp4-S1 (manual prune) Exp4-S2 (VIF prune)
R² TestR² Gap R² TestR² Gap R² TestR² Gap
BOD Grab
OLS---0.146+0.803-0.459+1.143
Ridge--+0.518-0.021+0.463+0.002
ElNet--+0.166+0.009+0.072+0.071

⚠ Overfitting note: Grab COD, Comp COD show R² gap > 0.25. Best is ranked by Test R² among non-overfit models (gap ≤ 0.25) where available. High-gap winners may reflect the model memorising 2021-2024 patterns absent in 2025 - prefer lower-gap alternatives for deployment.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Advanced Methods - ANN & Ensembles

Phase 9 evaluates advanced model architectures on the Exp3-S2 feature set, which was selected as the richest validated set (ElNet Test R²=0.684 Grab BOD, RF 0.504 Grab TSS - new records at the time).

Three architectures are tested: ANN (MLPRegressor) - failed; ~470 training samples insufficient for MLP (avg Test R²=−1.12). All targets selected alpha=1.0 (max regularisation). Not recommended.
Voting ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGBoost, equal weights) - original composition (Ridge+ElNet+RF) was corrected: Ridge duplicates ElNet's L2 penalty, making two of three voters collinear. XGBoost replaces Ridge for structural diversity (gradient boosting vs. bagging vs. regularised linear). All three voters pre-tuned via TimeSeriesSplit GridSearchCV/RandomizedSearchCV.
Stacking ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGB → Ridge meta, walk-forward OOF) - original KFold(n_splits=5) stacking had look-ahead bias: 2021 samples were predicted using base models trained on 2022-2024 data. Replaced with manual walk-forward OOF using TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5): each sample's OOF prediction uses only past data. ~83% OOF coverage per target; first ~17% excluded from meta-learner training.

▶ ANN (MLPRegressor) FAILED avg R²=−1.12

Exp3-S2 Features → ANN (MLPRegressor, StandardScaler pipeline)
Columns: Same as Exp3-S2 (25 features), StandardScaler + MLPRegressor, GridSearchCV on hidden_layer_sizes and alpha (TimeSeriesSplit)
Hypothesis: Tests whether a neural network can outperform regularised linear and tree models. Key challenge: ~470 training samples may be insufficient for a multi-layer perceptron.

▶ ANN Failure Post-Mortem avg Test R²=-1.123 · avg Gap=+1.865

Grid search: 5 architectures × 4 regularisation values = 20 combinations per target. Fixed: relu, adam, max_iter=2000, early_stopping=True (validation_fraction=0.10, n_iter_no_change=20). Tuning via TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=3). All targets selected alpha=1.0 (maximum regularisation).

6 of 8 targets have Test R² ≤ 0. ANN not recommended at current sample sizes.
Targetn_trainn_feat n/p Best archBest α Train R²Test R²R² Gap StatusΔ vs Voting
Comp pH6332426.4(1281.0, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (128, 64)0.564-6.2046.768CATASTROPHIC-6.410
Comp BOD4232715.7(641.0, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (64,)0.588-1.6052.193CATASTROPHIC-2.059
Grab pH8172335.5(1281.0, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (128,)0.759-1.5742.333CATASTROPHIC-1.795
Comp COD5151730.3(640.1, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (64,)0.653-0.2810.935FAILED+0.024
Grab BOD4702518.8(641.0, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (64,)0.750-0.1900.940FAILED-0.882
Comp TSS2902710.7(641.0, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (64,)0.8890.0540.835POOR-0.059
Grab TSS4692916.2(1281.0, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (128,)0.8360.3310.505MODERATE-0.147
Grab COD5651733.2(1280.001, 'mlp__hidden_layer_sizes': (128, 64)0.9000.4870.413MODERATE+0.055

n/p = training rows ÷ features. Red = n/p < 20 (underdetermined - high overfitting risk). MLPs typically need n/p ≥ 100-200 for stable generalisation on tabular data. Δ vs Voting = ANN Test R² − Phase 9 Voting Test R².

▶ Root-Cause Analysis

1. Sample-to-feature ratio. Worst case: Comp TSS - 290 training rows, 27 features → n/p = 10.7. MLPs require far more data than tree ensembles to generalise from small tabular datasets. Typical guidance: n/p ≥ 100-200 for stable MLP generalisation.

2. Temporal distribution shift. 2025 test data shows different distributional properties than 2021-2024 training data (Q4-Flow and DJF errors 2-3× higher - see Error Regime Decomposition). MLPs form highly non-linear decision boundaries that diverge further from shifted data than regularised linear models or bagged trees.

3. Early stopping did not prevent 2025 failure. Early stopping (validation_fraction=0.10, n_iter_no_change=20) monitors in-sample generalisation on the 2021-2024 fold, not 2025 generalisation. Gaps range from +0.41 (Grab COD) to +6.77 (Comp pH) despite early stopping - confirming that early stopping alone cannot address distributional shift.

4. Network capacity is not the bottleneck. Best architectures are small - (64,) or (128,) single hidden layer with α=1.0 for 6 of 8 targets. The grid search found that more capacity hurts; adding layers would worsen the gaps. The problem is generalisation, not expressiveness.

Future conditions for ANN. Dataset growth to n ≥ 3,000 per target and an LSTM architecture (explicit temporal modelling via hidden state rather than hand-crafted lags) are the recommended prerequisites before revisiting neural networks on this problem.

▶ Voting Ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGBoost) RECOMMENDED
Feature Set
Exp3-S2 Features → Voting Ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGBoost, equal weights)
Columns: Same as Exp3-S2 (25 features per target); VotingRegressor averaging ElasticNet, RandomForest, and XGBoost with equal weights. Replaces original Ridge+ElNet+RF - Ridge removed because it duplicates ElNet's L2 regularisation (redundant voter); XGBoost added for structural diversity (gradient boosting vs. bagging vs. linear).
Hypothesis: Ensemble averaging reduces variance through uncorrelated errors. ElNet: regularised linear with feature selection (L1). RF: bagging over decision trees, robust to outliers. XGB: boosted trees, captures residual non-linearities. Three structurally distinct inductive biases.
▶ Dataset Details (n_train / n_test / n_features)

▶ Stacking Ensemble (ElNet + RF + XGB → Ridge, walk-forward OOF) CONSISTENT - no leakage
Feature Set
Exp3-S2 Features → Stacking (ElNet + RF + XGB → Ridge meta, walk-forward OOF)
Columns: Same as Exp3-S2 (25 features); walk-forward stacking with TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5) OOF generation. Base: ElNet + RF + XGBoost. Meta-learner: Ridge (tuned alpha). First ~1/6 of training samples excluded from meta-learner training (no OOF fold coverage) - strictly no look-ahead bias. Base models retrained on full training set before inference.
Hypothesis: Replaces original KFold(n_splits=5) StackingRegressor which had look-ahead bias: predicting 2021 data using models trained on 2022-2024. Walk-forward OOF ensures the meta-learner learns how base models perform when projecting forward in time. Approx. 83% OOF coverage across all targets.

MdAE is the primary reliability metric for BOD/TSS; - indicates predictions not stored at row level (Phase 10 / Phase 11).

Negative R² Diagnosis - Variance Collapse vs Genuine Failure

R² = 1 − SS_res/SS_tot. A low-variance 2025 test set (σ-ratio < 0.5) shrinks the denominator and forces R² negative even when absolute accuracy is unchanged or improving. ΔMAE and ΔRMSE (2025 − 2024) give scale-grounded evidence: negative = model improved in absolute terms; positive = genuine deterioration. NRMSE = RMSE / (max − min) for scale-normalised cross-target comparison.

ModelTargetTest R² σ_trainσ_testσ-ratio MAE 2024MAE 2025ΔMAE RMSE 2024RMSE 2025ΔRMSE NRMSEDiagnosis
Stacking Comp COD -0.106 25.717 20.066 0.78 9.762 16.533 +6.771 12.307 21.102 +8.795 0.213 Genuine deterioration (MAE ×1.7 from 2024→2025)
Voting Comp COD -0.305 25.717 20.066 0.78 8.685 18.084 +9.399 11.131 22.922 +11.792 0.232 Genuine deterioration (MAE ×2.1 from 2024→2025)
Stacking Comp TSS -0.171 17.497 6.025 0.34 1.742 4.186 +2.443 3.153 6.518 +3.366 0.215 Mixed - σ-ratio=0.34, MAE ×2.4
Stacking Comp pH -0.326 0.284 0.204 0.72 0.256 0.189 -0.067 0.339 0.235 -0.104 0.163 MAE improved (0.256→0.189); R² negative due to variance collapse
Best Model in Phase 9 avg best Test R² = 0.319 · Grab avg 0.470 · Composite avg 0.167

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

ANN Dataset Exploration — Data-Volume Diagnostic

The Phase 9 ANN failed on Exp3-S2 datasets (avg Test R²=−1.12) due to insufficient training samples (~470 Grab, ~290 Composite). These runs test the same ANN architecture on earlier, data-richer experiment datasets to isolate whether the failure was data-volume limited or reflects a fundamental ANN limitation on this type of data.

Three datasets are tested: Exp1 (Inlet + COMMON, 9 features, ~1175 Grab / ~800 Composite rows — 2.5× more data), Exp2-Sub1 (Secondary + COMMON, 15 features, ~924 / ~740 rows), and Exp2-Sub2 (Inlet + Secondary + COMMON, 19 features, ~920 / ~733 rows). The hyperparameter grid includes larger architectures (256-128, 128-64-32 hidden layers) compared to Phase 9 ANN, appropriate for the larger sample counts.

▶ ANN — Exp1 Datasets (Inlet + COMMON, 9 features)
Feature Set
Exp1 Features → ANN (Inlet + COMMON, 9 features)
Columns: Inlet pH, Inlet BOD, Inlet COD, Inlet TSS (Grab or Composite) + Flow (MLD), Power Total (KW). StandardScaler + MLPRegressor, GridSearchCV on hidden_layer_sizes and alpha (TimeSeriesSplit). ~1175 Grab / ~800 Composite training rows.
Hypothesis: Phase 9 ANN failed with ~470 Grab training samples. Exp1 has 2.5× more data with simpler features. Tests whether ANN failure was data-volume limited. Same architecture as Phase 9 ANN; only dataset changed.

ANN Dataset Exploration — Key Findings

  • Inlet features alone (Exp1, 9 features) are insufficient for the ANN — avg Test R²=−5.6, worse than Phase 9 (Exp3-S2, −1.1). More data cannot compensate for missing secondary process signal.
  • Secondary features unlock positive Grab R² for the first time — Exp2-Sub1 ANN (Secondary + COMMON, 12 features, ~924 rows) achieves Grab BOD +0.20, Grab TSS +0.27. This is the only dataset × architecture combination where the ANN produces positive generalisation on Grab targets.
  • Adding inlet to secondary data (Exp2-Sub2) does not help composites — Comp BOD collapses from −0.11 (Exp2-S1) to −1.49 (Exp2-S2). The ANN overfits more with 16 features than with 12 on the same 733 composite rows.
  • Composite targets fail for the ANN on every dataset tested — all composite test R² are negative across Exp1, Exp2-Sub1, Exp2-Sub2, and Phase 9 (Exp3-S2). The pattern is consistent: composite measurements are temporally noisier and the ANN cannot capture the distributional shift from training to 2025.
  • Conclusion — the ANN failure is not data-volume limited — tripling the training rows (Exp1: 1175 vs Exp3-S2: 470) did not rescue performance. The binding constraint is the feature set: secondary process data is a prerequisite for positive ANN generalisation on Grab targets. Even with secondary features and adequate data (~924 rows), the ANN substantially underperforms the Voting ensemble (avg Grab R²≈0.20 vs Voting 0.287 overall). The ANN is not recommended for any target on this dataset.

Feature Engineering

Phase 10 applies feature engineering (log transforms, interaction terms, outlier flags) on top of the Exp3-S2 feature set. Phase 10 (full) applies this uniformly to all targets. Phase 10b (selective) applies FE only to grab targets after discovering that composite datasets overfit severely with the expanded feature count.

▶ Feature Engineering - Full (All Targets) COMPOSITE OVERFIT
Feature Set
Exp3-S2 + Full Feature Engineering - All Targets (37-52 features)
Columns: Exp3-S2 base + log1p transforms of skewed cols + pairwise interaction terms (key pairs) + IQR outlier indicator flags; applied uniformly to all 8 targets
Hypothesis: Feature engineering to capture non-linear relationships and reduce skewness. Applied uniformly to test if it universally helps. Hypothesis: log transforms help BOD/COD/TSS (right-skewed).

▶ Phase 10b - Selective Feature Engineering (Grab FE / Composite Base) CURRENT BEST
Feature Set
Selective Feature Engineering - Grab: Full FE / Composite: Base Exp3-S2
Columns: Grab targets: full FE (37-52 features each). Composite targets: base Exp3-S2 features only (17-27 features) - no engineering applied to avoid overfitting on small composite n.
Hypothesis: Addresses Phase 10 finding: FE caused severe overfitting on composite datasets (n_train ≈ 290). Selective approach preserves FE gains on grab targets while stabilising composites.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Temporal Features + log1p Targets

Phase 11 adds temporal lag features (lag 1/3/7-day) and a 7-day rolling mean to inlet, Flow, and Power columns, plus a log1p target transform on BOD/COD/TSS with Duan smearing back-transform. Feature expansion is restricted to inlet+Flow+Power columns to avoid the catastrophic overfitting seen when applying temporal features to all columns (which would produce ~128 features on ~290 composite rows).

Key wins: Grab BOD (Ridge 0.656, gap −0.095) and Grab COD (XGB 0.464, gap +0.057 - new Grab COD high with an honest gap). Key losses: all composite targets deteriorate, especially Comp TSS (Ridge −1.04, gap +1.82). Conclusion: temporal features help Grab targets; composites lack the sample size to benefit. CV_R² and Gap_gen = CV_R² − Test_R² are recorded per model for distribution-shift diagnosis.

▶ Temporal Features - All Models
Feature Set
Exp3-S2 + Temporal Lags + log1p Target Transform (50-58 features)
Columns: Base Exp3-S2 features + lag 1/3/7-day + 7-day calendar rolling mean applied to inlet + Flow + Power columns only (~50-58 total features per target). BOD/COD/TSS targets log1p-transformed; pH untransformed. Back-transform via Duan smearing: expm1(ŷ) × mean(exp(training residuals)).
Hypothesis: Daily wastewater quality has temporal autocorrelation - yesterday's influent predicts today's effluent better than cross-sectional features alone. Lag expansion restricted to inlet+Flow+Power columns to avoid catastrophic overfitting on composite datasets (n≈290 rows). log1p targets reduce right-skew in BOD/COD/TSS distributions.

Selection criteria: highest Test R² among models with R² gap ≤ 0.25. Alternatives: (a) use harmonic mean of Train+Test R² to penalise both under- and overfitting; (b) rank by RMSE if absolute error scale matters; (c) for deployment, cap gap at 0.15 for stricter stability.

Overfit-Aware Model Selection

Three complementary rules re-rank every (target × experiment) row beyond naive max(Test R²): gap-adjusted score penalises overfit beyond a 10-pt tolerance; the one-SE rule picks the smallest-gap model within 0.03 R² of the top; the Pareto frontier lists all models not dominated on (R²_test ↑, |gap| ↓). RMSE and MAE are shown alongside R² for operational interpretability - they rank identically to R² within a target but carry real units (mg/L or pH) that relate to discharge consent limits.

Why re-rank?

On this dataset (n_test ≈ 160-280 for the held-out 2025 year), Test R² alone is noisy. A model with R²=0.43 and gap=0.88 is not equivalent to one with R²=0.40 and gap=0.02 - the former is memorising.

Rules applied

Gap-adjusted: score = R²_test − 0.5 · max(0, |gap| − 0.1). Penalises overfit beyond a 10-pt tolerance.

One-SE rule: among models within 0.05 R² of the top, pick the smallest |gap|.

Pareto frontier: models not dominated on (R²_test ↑, |gap| ↓).

RMSE and MAE are shown alongside R² for each rule's winner - they carry the same ranking as R² within a target but are operationally interpretable (mg/L for BOD/COD/TSS, pH units for pH).

Headline

Naive winner differs from gap-adjusted / one-SE winner in 5 / 8 global target rows and 32 / 208 per-experiment rows.

Global best per target (across all experiments)

All rules agree, gap ≤ 0.25All rules agree, gap > 0.25 - cautionGap-adj picks a different model (blue = recommended)

Click ⓘ on any Gap-adj winner for the reasoning behind that selection.

Source: 1112 model evaluations across 26 experiments.

2025 Residual Decomposition by Operational Regime

Decomposes 2025 residuals for the Phase 9 Voting (ElNet+RF+XGB) model across four operational axes: Flow quartile, Weekday vs Weekend, Season (DJF/MAM/JJA/SON), and Inlet Load quartile. Quartile thresholds are fit on the training set (2021-2024) only. Headline finding: error concentrates in Q4 high-flow and DJF winter regimes (2-3× overall MAE) for every BOD/TSS target - predictions during hydraulic or thermal stress should be flagged as low-confidence.

No error decomposition data available.

Comp COD - Persistent Failure Diagnostic

Effluent COD (Composite) is the only target where no model generalises across any experiment or phase (best Test R² = 0.154 across 26 experiment variants). Phase 9 Voting MAE doubled from 8.7 mg/L (2024 in-sample) to 18.1 mg/L (2025 test). Variance is not collapsed (σ-ratio = 0.78), which rules out the low-variance artefact that drives negative R² in Comp pH and Comp TSS. This section documents the distribution shift, the performance panorama across every experiment, and the evidence for a 2025 process change as the likely root cause.

Global best Test R² across all experiments: 0.154. Every regularisation strategy, feature set expansion, and engineering approach has been tried; none breaks through to meaningful generalisation on 2025.

Root Cause Analysis

1. Genuine 2025 process change - not a modelling artefact. MAE doubled (8.7 → 18.1 mg/L) while σ-ratio = 0.78, confirming the 2025 test set has comparable variance to training. The model is not hitting a low-variance wall - it is predicting the wrong values. This is the signature of a non-stationarity in the plant's COD removal mechanism that was absent during the 2021-2024 training window.

2. Inlet COD is a weak proxy for effluent COD under the current conditions. Composite effluent COD depends on secondary treatment efficiency, which is governed by MLSS, SVI, and aeration conditions - features with 35-50% missingness on composite rows (CONSIDER tier). When these are absent, the model falls back to inlet + flow, whose MI with effluent Comp COD is low (<0.15 on training data).

3. Small composite sample size amplifies memorisation. Composite targets have only ~515-633 training rows after dropna. With 17-32 features the effective rows-per-feature ratio is 16-37 - below the commonly cited heuristic of 50. Even heavily regularised models (ElNet α=10) risk encoding 2022-era COD spikes that do not recur in 2025.

4. Feature engineering and temporal lags do not help. Phase 10 full FE: best R² = −0.008 (Ridge). Phase 10b selective FE: best = −0.051. Phase 11 temporal lags: best = +0.107 (ElNet, Phase 11) but 2025 MAE remains ~17 mg/L. Every additional feature increases the risk of learning training-specific patterns without improving generalisation.

5. Recommended next steps. (a) Flag Comp COD predictions as unreliable in any operational dashboard until 2025 data is incorporated into training. (b) Collect causal features with higher priority: secondary sludge age (SRT), MLSS, and effluent turbidity have high missingness now but directly govern COD removal - their availability on composite measurement days would be the single highest leverage improvement. (c) Retrain once ≥ 90 new 2025 rows are available (roughly 3 months of composite measurements) and test whether the new regime is stable enough for a combined 2021-2025 model. (d) As an interim measure, a shallow decision tree (max depth 3) trained only on 2024-2025 data is likely to outperform any model trained on the full 2021-2024 window for near-term operational use.
